title: STANDARD FOR GRAPEFRUITS CXS 219-1999 Adopted in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR GRAPEFRUITS CXS 219-1999 Adopted in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.
sections.summary: Document heading identifying the Standard for Grapefruits (CXS 219-1999) and listing adoption/amendment years (1999; amended 2005, 2011); not a substantive regulatory provision.

Defines scope: the Standard applies to commercial varieties of grapefruit (Citrus paradisi) supplied fresh to consumers after preparation and packaging and excludes grapefruits intended for industrial processing.

Sets quality provisions for grapefruits covering minimum requirements (whole, sound, clean, practically pest- and bruise-free, firm, free of abnormal moisture/foreign smell or temperature damage), maturity and colouring rules, and classification into Extra, Class I and Class II with specified defect allowances and tolerances to ensure suitability for transport and delivery.

Specifies minimum requirements for grapefruits — they must be whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests/damage/bruising, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign odours/tastes, firm and free of temperature damage; must reach appropriate development/ripeness to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition, with a minimum juice content of 35% and colouring typical of the variety (greenish fruits allowed if meeting minima; degreening permitted if organoleptic characteristics are not altered).

sections.section: 2.1.1 The grapefruits must have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness in accordance with criteria proper to the variety and/or commercial type and to the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Grapefruits must have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness according to the variety/commercial type and the area where grown.

sections.section: The development and condition of the grapefruits must be such as to enable them:
sections.summary: The development and condition of the grapefruits must enable them to withstand transport and handling and to arrive in satisfactory condition at the place of destination.
Maturity requirement: minimum juice content is calculated relative to total fruit weight and set at 35%.
Maturity requirement: minimum juice content is calculated relative to total fruit weight and set at 35%.

Colouring must be typical of the variety (greenish fruits allowed if they meet minimum requirements; red-pulp varieties may have reddish rind patches) and grapefruits meeting ripeness minima may be degreened provided this does not modify other organoleptic characteristics.
States that grapefruits are classified into three classes (Extra, Class I and Class II), which are defined in the following sections.

'Extra' class: grapefruits must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial defects that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation (note clarifies 'colour' refers to characteristic colour, not discoloration from pests/blemishes).

'Extra' class: grapefruits must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial defects that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation (note clarifies 'colour' refers to characteristic colour, not discoloration from pests/blemishes).

'Extra' class: grapefruits must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial defects that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation (note clarifies 'colour' refers to characteristic colour, not discoloration from pests/blemishes).

Class I: grapefruits of good quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type, may have slight defects in shape, colouring, skin formation or slight healed mechanical damage and up to one-fifth skin discolouration from rust mite/melanoses/blemishes provided these do not affect general appearance, quality, keeping, presentation or the pulp.

Class II: includes grapefruits not qualifying for higher classes but meeting minimum requirements, allowing more pronounced defects in shape, colouring, healed superficial wounds or mechanical damage, rough skin and up to two-fifths skin discolouration from rust mite/melanoses/blemishes, provided the defects do not affect the pulp and the fruit retain essential quality, keeping and presentation characteristics.

Defines sizing by maximum equatorial diameter with a size-code table, allows packing by count (permitting a size range within two adjacent codes if uniformity retained), excludes fruit under 70 mm, and requires size uniformity except for bulk bins and individual non-rigid consumer sale packages where the max size difference may span three consecutive sizes.

Allows specified tolerances in each package/lot: class-specific quality tolerances (Extra up to 5% not meeting class but meeting Class I, Class I up to 10% not meeting class but meeting Class II, Class II up to 10% not meeting class or minima with up to 5% of slight unhealed/damaged fruit) and size tolerances of 10% to the adjacent size (with a bulk-consignment exception for fruit ≥70 mm).

Quality tolerances by class: Extra allows 5% by number/weight not meeting Extra but meeting Class I (or its tolerances); Class I allows 10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II (or its tolerances); Class II allows 10% not meeting Class II or the minima (excluding produce unfit for consumption) with up to 5% permitted for slight unhealed damage, dry cuts or soft/shrivelled fruit.
Extra class quality tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight may not satisfy Extra class requirements provided they meet Class I or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
Class I quality tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight may not satisfy Class I requirements provided they meet Class II or, exceptionally, fall within Class II tolerances.

Class II quality tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight may fail to meet Class II or minimum requirements (excluding produce affected by rotting or otherwise unfit for consumption), and within this 10% a maximum of 5% may show slight superficial unhealed damage, dry cuts or be soft and shrivelled.

Size tolerances: for all classes up to 10% by number or weight may correspond to the size immediately above or below that on the package, and for bulk consignments the 10% tolerance applies only to fruit with diameter not less than 70 mm.

Sets presentation requirements for grapefruits including uniformity by origin/variety/quality/size/colour, packing with new/clean/food‑grade materials using non‑toxic inks, container quality/hygiene/ventilation/resistance, and class‑specific permitted forms of presentation with size‑scale rules.
Each package or bulk lot must be uniform and contain only grapefruits of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality, size and colour, and the visible portion must represent the whole contents.

Grapefruits must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean, food‑grade materials (including suitable recycled food‑grade material), avoiding external/internal damage, non‑toxic inks/glues for printed materials, and in accordance with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44‑1995).

Containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength requirements to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of grapefruits and be free from foreign matter and odour (food‑grade recycled material permitted).

Permitted presentation forms are: (a) aligned in layers by size (mandatory for 'Extra' class, optional for Classes I and II); (b) non‑aligned in packaging or bulk within one transport means with maximum size difference equal to the sum of three consecutive size classes (allowed for Classes I and II); (c) bulk with only minimum size requirement (Class II only); and (d) individual consumer packages ≤5 kg, with mandatory size scales when packed by count and size‑variation limits when packed by weight.
Heading only introducing the marking and labelling provisions for the standard (no substantive detail in this heading).
Consumer package labelling must meet the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) plus the specific provisions that follow in this standard.

Non‑retail packages must bear legible, indelible particulars grouped on the same side and visible from the outside or appear in accompanying documents; for bulk transport these particulars must be on the shipping documents.
Identification must include the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an optional identification code.
If contents are not visible, the package must state the name of the produce, may state the variety or commercial type, and where appropriate indicate 'pink' or 'red'.
Labelling must show the country of origin and may optionally include the district or national/regional/local place name where grown.

Commercial identification must indicate class, size (size code or minimum/maximum diameter in mm), optional net weight, and when fruit are packed by count the applicable size code(s) and number of fruit (with specific rules when counts span adjacent size codes).

An official inspection mark is optional; where a code mark is used, the packer/dispatcher reference must be shown near the code and some national laws require explicit name and address in addition to or instead of a code.
Grapefruits must comply with the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Preparation and handling of the produce should follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53‑2003) and other relevant Codex texts, and comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21‑1997.
